parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to breeze through unexpected changes without a second thought, but lately even the smallest shift in my routine leaves me completely rattled. I catch myself holding my breath over things that never used to bother me at all. My chest gets tight and my thoughts start spinning out of control before I can even catch myself. It is just a weird feeling to watch my own reactions spiral over absolutely nothing. I keep waiting for it to pass, but the tension just lingers in the background.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly disorienting to feel like a stranger to your own reactions. When you’ve spent most of your life being the person who can "roll with the punches," suddenly feeling fragile or reactive can feel like a betrayal by your own mind.

That sensation of holding your breath and the tightness in your chest is your nervous system stuck in a state of high alert. It’s as if your internal "alarm system" has become hyper-sensitive; where it used to only go off for actual emergencies, it’s now screaming at a slight change in the weather or a shift in your schedule.

Often, this happens not because of the "small" things themselves, but because of **cumulative stress.** If you’ve been carrying a heavy load for a long time—even if you’ve been doing it well—your capacity to absorb new changes eventually hits a ceiling. The "lingering tension" you’re feeling is often a sign that your baseline level of stress is already so high that there’s no room left for even a tiny ripple.

When you find yourself in that spiral, it might help to try a few things to signal to your body that you are actually safe:

*   **Exhale longer than you inhale:** When your chest gets tight, your body is primed for "fight or flight." A long, slow exhale (like you’re breathing through a straw) physically forces your heart rate to slow down and tells your brain the "threat" is over.
*   **Name the sensation, not the "why":** Instead of wondering why you’re spiraling over "nothing," try saying, *"My chest is tight and my heart is fast right now."* It takes the judgment out of it and helps you observe the feeling rather than being consumed by it.
*   **Lower the bar:** If your routine changes, give yourself permission to be "rattled." Sometimes the secondary stress—being frustrated that you’re upset—is actually heavier than the original change itself.

Have there been any big shifts in your life lately, or has it just been a slow build-up of pressure over time? Sometimes just acknowledging that you’re "full" can help let a little bit of that steam out.

turns.user_message: What really gets to me is how this reaction has become my new normal instead of just a passing phase. I used to just shake off that nervous energy, but now it builds up until I feel completely overwhelmed by my own heartbeat. Every little surprise feels like a warning sign that something is about to go wrong. I try to ground myself by taking deep breaths, but the panic just keeps creeping back in anyway. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own body for signs of trouble.
turns.model_response:
It is incredibly draining to live in a state of "high alert" where your own body feels like a source of danger rather than a safe place to be. What you’re describing—that shift from a passing feeling to a "new normal"—is a very heavy burden to carry.

When your nervous system stays in a state of hyper-vigilance for too long, it starts to treat every small sensation or surprise as a threat. It’s like a smoke alarm that has become so sensitive it goes off every time you try to cook. Even though you know you’re safe, the alarm is deafening, and it’s hard to ignore the physical toll of that constant noise.

The frustration you feel about deep breathing not "working" is also very common. When you are already in the middle of that creeping panic, sometimes focusing on your breath can actually make you *more* aware of your heartbeat and your body, which feeds back into the cycle of monitoring yourself for trouble.

Since you mentioned that monitoring your body is the most exhausting part, have you found anything that helps pull your focus *outward* into the room around you, rather than inward? Sometimes, when the body feels like a "warning sign," looking at something neutral—like the texture of a rug or the sound of traffic outside—can provide a tiny bit of distance from that internal intensity.

Please be gentle with yourself. You aren't doing anything wrong; your body is just trying very hard to protect you, even if its methods are currently making things much harder for you.

turns.user_message: I honestly do not recognize myself anymore because this constant state of alert has completely taken over my everyday life. I used to laugh at people who got worked up over minor details, but now I am the one trapped in that exact cycle. My mind races so fast that I feel like I am about to jump out of my own skin. Every single moment feels heavy and urgent, leaving me completely drained before the day even really starts. I just want to go back to feeling steady and calm without having to fight for it.
turns.model_response:
I am so sorry you are going through this. It sounds incredibly exhausting to feel like your own mind and body have become unfamiliar territory. 

What you’re describing—that "constant state of alert"—is often the body’s survival mechanism (the fight-or-flight response) getting stuck in the "on" position. When that happens, your nervous system stops being able to distinguish between a minor detail and a genuine threat, which is why everything feels heavy, urgent, and draining. It’s not a character flaw or a loss of who you are; it’s a physiological state of high-alert exhaustion.

It makes sense that you feel a sense of grief for the person you used to be—the version of you who felt steady and could laugh things off. That contrast only adds to the frustration because you remember what "calm" feels like, but it feels out of reach right now.

When you are in that "jumping out of your skin" headspace, your brain is moving too fast for logic to catch up. Since you mentioned wanting to feel steady again, here are a few ways to gently signal to your nervous system that you are safe, without having to "think" your way out of it:

*   **Temperature Shock:** If your mind is racing uncontrollably, splash ice-cold water on your face or hold an ice cube in your hand. This forces your nervous system to "reset" and pulls you out of your head and back into your body.
*   **The "Exhale" Rule:** When we are anxious, we tend to take short, shallow breaths. Try to make your exhale twice as long as your inhale (e.g., breathe in for 4, out for 8). This physically stimulates the vagus nerve to tell your brain to slow down.
*   **Lower the Bar:** On days when you feel drained before the day starts, give yourself permission to do the bare minimum. The "urgency" you feel is often a lie told by anxiety; most things can actually wait.

Have you been able to talk to a professional or a doctor about this? When the "alert" phase lasts this long and starts changing your personality, it can be really hard to break the cycle entirely on your own. You deserve to feel like yourself again, and you shouldn't have to fight this hard just to exist. 

How long has this "high alert" feeling been dominating things for you?
